coredata.dc:title: Reprecipitation induced isolation of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks as a green emissive UV light filter
coredata.dc:creator: Uthirakumar, Periyayya
Devendiran, M.
Park, Jee Hye
Lee, In-Hwan
coredata.dc:description: A strong green emissive UV shielding polymer film has been prepared by incorporating different weight fraction of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks into a polymer matrix. A routine reprecipitation method is introduced to isolate ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks for better dispersion in a polymer matrix. As-prepared polymer film shows an excellent UV shielding with increasing content of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. About 30% of UV shielding was observed with 7.5 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, while the maximum UV shielding of 80 and 83% was witnessed in a polymer film containing 25 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks under a UV lamp and direct daylight sources, respectively. The UV-shielding capability of polymer film was further verified by testing of UV light induced rhodamine B degradation and compared to pure polymer film. Furthermore, due to a down-conversion of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, a strong green emission was observed of converting high energy UV radiation into green emission, upon illumination of UV light. Thus, the development of green emissive UV shielding polymer film can be recommended to use as a packaging film, emulsion, paints and protective coating etc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: All-inorganic perovskite
Ligand-free Cs4PbBr6
UV-shielding
Polymer film
Nanodisks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Test for UV shielding capacity of polymer film on rhodamine B degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic representation for the preparation of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks via reprecipitation induced nano-crystallization method. Photographs of each stage were inserted and FESEM images of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 crystals before and after reprecipitation method.
(a) XRD pattern, core level (b) Cs 3d, (c) Pb 4f, (d) Br 3d XPS spectra, (e) UV visible absorbance and PL emission and (f) Tauc graph of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks.
Low and high magnified (a–c) FESEM images and (d) TEM image of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks.
FESEM images with EDS spectral data of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks(a,b) on entire area of image and (c, d) area selected on the surface of nanodisk.
A schematic of testing UV shielding tendency of polymer film dispersed ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks with the help of a digital lux meter.
UV shielding tendency of polymer film with respect to different weight percentage of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks under (a) a UV lamp, (b) daylight source.

(a) low and (b) high magnified optical microscopic images of polymer film containing ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. A schematic illustration (c) and (d) UV absorption spectra of UV light induced RhB dye degradation covering PP-0 and PP-5 polymer films [Inset: the photographic image during the dye degradation experiment].
(a) A schematic illustration and (b) the plausible mechanism for the down-conversion of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks dispersed polymer film.

head.ce:title: Reprecipitation induced isolation of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks as a green emissive UV light filter

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Korea University, Seoul 02841, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices [Nano-COED], Department of Chemistry, Sona College of Technology, Salem, Tamil Nadu, 636005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanoscience Center for Optoelectronic and Energy Devices [Nano-COED]

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Korea University, Seoul 02841, South Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A routine reprecipitation method is introduced to isolate ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks for better dispersion in a polymer matrix. As-prepared polymer film shows an excellent UV shielding of 80% was witnessed in a polymer film containing 25 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks under a UV lamp. The UV-shielding capability of polymer film was further verified by testing of UV light induced rhodamine B degradation and compared to pure polymer film. Furthermore, the polymer film emits a strong green due to a down-conversion property of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, upon illumination of UV light. Thus, the development of green emissive UV shielding polymer film can be recommended to use as a packaging film, emulsion, paints and protecting coating etc.

A strong green emissive UV shielding polymer film has been prepared by incorporating different weight fraction of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks into a polymer matrix. A routine reprecipitation method is introduced to isolate ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks for better dispersion in a polymer matrix. As-prepared polymer film shows an excellent UV shielding with increasing content of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. About 30% of UV shielding was observed with 7.5 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, while the maximum UV shielding of 80 and 83% was witnessed in a polymer film containing 25 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks under a UV lamp and direct daylight sources, respectively. The UV-shielding capability of polymer film was further verified by testing of UV light induced rhodamine B degradation and compared to pure polymer film. Furthermore, due to a down-conversion of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, a strong green emission was observed of converting high energy UV radiation into green emission, upon illumination of UV light. Thus, the development of green emissive UV shielding polymer film can be recommended to use as a packaging film, emulsion, paints and protective coating etc.

ce:section.ce:para: The ultraviolet (UV) light is a high energy electromagnetic radiation comprising of UV-A (320–400 nm), UV-B (280–320 nm) and UV-C (200–280 nm) [1]. Amongst, UV-A and UV-B rays create serious damage in DNA, cataracts, cancer, dyes, pigments, polymers and woods etc [2–7]. The major reason for high intensity of UV light penetration to the earth surface is due to extensive destroying of ozone layer in the past decades [8]. Thus, to protect the UV sensitive materials, it is necessary develop UV light absorbing materials to impart higher life span. Although a great success has been made in preparing verity of UV shielding materials, it requires a better medium to disperse these materials for practical application, preferably a polymer. Hence, many recent reports deal the development of different types of UV shielding materials dispersed polymer films including inorganic [9–11], organic [12–14] and composite materials [15–18]. In continuation, over the past few decades, optically transparent UV shielding composite materials were recommended for fabricating windows, contact lens, glasses and protective film to shield UV light [19,20]. Such composite film imparts the physical flexibility and ease of processing for fabricating devices. In comparison to organic materials, inorganic UV shielding materials are more favorable in practical applications due to their high optical and chemical stability, wide shielding range and low toxicity [21,22].

In the recent years, all inorganic cesium lead halide perovskites are emerging as new generation functional materials for various optoelectronic applications such as light emitting diodes, solar cells, lasers, and photodectors [23–26]. Zero-dimensional Cs4PbBr6 has been identified as an efficient green luminescent materials due to the inclusion of CsPbBr3 or Br vacancies [27,28]. Besides, Cs4PbBr6 crystals has received a significant interest in the field of wide bandgap semiconductor materials, due to its large bandgap, which have particular application in the fields of UV detection [29–31]. Specifically, in addition to larger bandgap, Cs4PbBr6 crystals exhibit remarkable optical property of absorbing entire UV light, capable to extend their application in the area of UV shielding materials. Thus, we proposed to use the Cs4PbBr6 crystals as UV shielding materials to protect the UV sensitive materials, for the first time. Furthermore, due to the down-conversion property of Cs4PbBr6 crystals, the polymer film produces a strong green emission. Therefore, developing Cs4PbBr6 based UV shielding material with a strong green emissive polymer film can have a broad industrial application including packaging films, emulsion, paints and protecting coating etc.

In this work, we report the fabrication of green emissive UV shielding polymer film containing different weight fraction of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. In contrary to the ligand assisted reprecipitation technique [32] and single atomic supersaturated recrystallization method [33], we incorporated a routing reprecipitation process to isolate ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks without the support of ligands and surfactants. The UV-shielding tendency was measured and verified by investigating the degradation trend of UV-light induced dye. The present study could of first report to focus on the fabrication of a strong green emissive ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks dispersed polymer film as a UV filter.

Cesium bromide (CsBr, 98%), lead bromide (PbBr2, 98%), poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), rhodamine B, analytical grade N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, and isopropyl alcohol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. The carbon quantum dot covered ZnO was prepared and used as photocatalyst for dye degradation [18].

A routine reprecipitation method is introduced to isolate nanocrystalline structure of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks [27,34]. Here, a good solvent, double distilled water is used to dissolve the Cs precursor and reprecipitated with an isopropyl alcohol, a poor solvent. Typically, 0.63 g of CsBr was completely dissolved in 3 mL of water and reprecipitated with 50 ml isopropyl alcohol. The white precipitate of CsBr was filtered and dispersed in 10 mL of DMF in a 50 mL beaker on a magnetic stirrer at 120 °C. In parallel, 0.36 g of PbBr2 was dissolved in 10 mL of DMF by sonication. A pellucid PbBr2 solution was quickly injected into Cs precursor dispersion at 120 °C and stirred for 5 min. The reddish orange Cs4PbBr6 crystals were filtered and washed with DMF (5 mL). After DMF washing the color of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 crystals was changed from reddish orange into green, and collected after filtration for further processing.

The ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks dispersed polymer films were prepared as a function of increasing content of Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks in a PMMA matrix, based on earlier report [20]. Briefly, 7.5 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks taken in a beaker containing 1 g of PMMA dissolved in 30 ml of acetone. For effective dispersion, the mixture was kept in a sonic bath for 5 min and continued 60 min of stirring on a magnetic stirrer. The viscous polymer dispersion was transferred into a stainless steel cup and stand for 48 h, for complete curing. The polymer film containing 7.5 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks perovskite materials was labelled as PP-1. Likewise, other polymer films, such as PP-2, PP-3, PP-4, and PP-5 were prepared in the same way by mixing 10, 15, 20, and 25 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, respectively. In addition, a pure polymer film PP-0 was also prepared without ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks material, for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test for UV shielding capacity of polymer film on rhodamine B degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The UV shielding capacity of polymer film was tested according to a reported method with some modifications [16]. Typically, 10 mg carbon quantum dot decorated ZnO (C-ZnO) photocatalyst was dispersed in 30 mL rhodamine B (RhB) aqueous solution (0.01 g/L) in a 50-mL beaker concealed with aluminium foil. The solution was stirred for 30 min in dark to establish a complete adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Before UV light illumination, PP-0 and PP-5 polymer film was roofed on top of the beaker, ensuring total coverage. The photocatalytic degradation of RhB was performed under UV light irradiation from a UV lamp (18 W). After 3 h, dye solution was centrifuged to remove photocatalyst and the absorbance at 552 nm of the solution was determined to calculate the percentage of dye degradation.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were analyzed by a Rigaku (D/Max-2500 V/PC) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1546 nm) monochromated by a nickel filter. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), elemental mapping and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) measurements were performed on Hitachi S‐4800 microscope, working at 15 kV. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai TF20) was operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 KeV. The optical property was analyzed with an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu). Photoluminescent (PL) spectra was obtained using luminescence spectrometer (Aminco Bowman II, Thermo Spectronic). The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was determined using photon counting spectrofluorometer equipped with an integrating sphere. The optical microscope was used to measure the dispersion of materials in polymer matrix. The UV shielding property was measured by a UV light Digital meter (UV-340 A, Lutron). Two different light sources, a UV lamp (18 W, 365 nm) and direct daylight (Sunlight, 9-27th November 2018) were used to investigate the UV-shielding property.

In general, compared to the successful ligand assisted synthesis of Cs4PbBr6 quantum dots/nanocrystals, it is hard to prepare nanocrystalline Cs4PbBr6 crystals without the support of ligands and surfactants [23–27]. It is obvious to note that the ligand or surfactant molecules are to be stabilized the precursor for crystallizing Cs4PbBr6 quantum dots [35,36]. In contrary, we are demonstrating a new method to prepare ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals by adopting a reprecipitation method without the support of ligands and surfactants. Here, a routine reprecipitation method was chosen as a bottom-up technique to prepare Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals [27,34], via a solvent exchange process from good to poor solvent. An appropriate good solvent was chosen to dissolve the target materials, and reprecipitated with the help of a poor solvent, in which the target materials are insoluble. Fig. 1 shows the schematic illustration of preparing ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks by introducing a reprecipitation method. Being CsBr is insoluble in most of organic solvents, it settled down at the bottom of the sample vial. Hence, to improve the dispersion of Cs precursor in DMF, a reprecipitation method was employed by dissolving Cs precursor in double distilled water and re-precipitated with a poor solvent, isopropyl alcohol. The isolated Cs precursor showed a better dispersion in DMF, as clearly seen in the photographs at Fig. 1. For better clarity, a comparative photograph of before and after reprecipitation of Cs precursor in DMF was displayed in Fig. S1. The noticeable changes in size and shape was observed after isolation of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals, the respective FESEM images of before and after reprecipitation method, as shown in Fig. 1. Here, the regular micro-structured crystals were completely converted into nanostructured ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks by introducing a reprecipitation method.

Fig. 2 a depicts the X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. The X-ray diffraction peaks at 12.6°, 12.9°, 20.0°, 22.4°, 25.5°, 27.5°, 28.5°, 29.3°, 30.2°, 37.4°, 43.6° and 52.1° were corresponding to (012), (110), (113), (030), (024), (131), (214), (312), (233), (315), (152), and (048) planes, revealing rhombohedral phase (JPCDS No. 73-2478) of Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks [37]. The appearance of sharp intensity X-ray peaks represent the high crystalline structure of Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. However, in addition to rhombohedral phase of Cs4PbBr6 crystals, a single peak appeared at 21.5° corresponding to the (110) plane of orthorhombic CsPbBr3 phase (JPCDS No. 01-072-7929) [27]. This result confirms the inclusion of CsPbBr3 phase in the ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed to investigate the surface states of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, as shown in Fig. 2. The survey scan and core level XPS spectra confirm the existence of Cs, Pb, and Br in ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, as given in Fig. 2(b–d) and Fig S2 (supporting information). The core level Cs 3d spectra consists of two peaks, Cs 3d5/2 and Cs 3d3/2 appeared with the binding energies of 724 eV and 738 eV, respectively. The binding energies of 138 eV and 143 eV are corresponding to Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2, respectively. Similarly, the core level Br 3d XPS peak was deconvoluted into fit by two peaks with the binding energies of 68.7 eV and 67.7 eV, as shown in Fig. 2d. The binding energies of 68.7 eV and 67.7 eV can be attributed to the Br atoms of CsBr impurity or corner-sharing [PbBr6]4− of CsPbBr3 crystals and Br atoms in isolated [PbBr6]4− of Cs4PbBr6 crystals, respectively. However, the pure CsPbBr3 crystals produce a single peak with a binding energy of 67.7 eV [7]. Both XRD and XPS studies confirm the inclusion of CsPbBr3 phase in the isolated ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks.

The optical properties of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks were investigated by measuring absorption and photoluminescent spectrophotometry. Fig. 2e shows the UV–vis absorbance and photoluminescent emission spectra of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. The UV absorbance and emission peak maximum of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks were appeared at 320 nm and 517 nm, respectively [37,38]. A broad absorption peak centered at 320 nm is possibly due to the unique isolated PbBr6 octahedron layers in the structure of Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks [39], while, an absorption peak at 520 nm due to the CsPbBr3 inclusion [39,40]. Tauc plot was drawn to determine the band gap value of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, and it was found that two different band-gap values at 2.25 and 3.6 eV were noticed, respectively to the Cs4PbBr6 and CsPbBr3 phases, as shown in Fig. 2f. These results strongly authenticated that the inclusion of CsPbBr3 phase in nanocrystalline structure of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. Thus, a characteristic PL emission peak appeared at 517 nm is consistent with recent reports due to inclusion of CsPbBr3 phase in the ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks with a maximum PL quantum yield of ˜ 43.3% [39–41]. Also, the full width half maximum (FWHM) of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 was found to be 25.9 nm. The lower FWHM value represents the nanostructured ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks with a high crystalline structure, compared to bulk crystals [27]. In addition, PL decay curve of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 emission at 519 nm was collected and fitted with a biexponential function, as shown in Fig. S3 (supporting information). The fitting function consists a longer (τ1) and a shorter (τ2) lifetime representing surface defects radiative recombination and an interior CsPbBr3 state recombination, respectively [36].

Fig. 3 shows the low and high magnified FESEM and TEM images of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. The FESEM images confirm the presence of disk shaped ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 crystals. The thickness of individual nanodisks were noticed between 100 and 300 nm, with few micrometers long in diameter. In addition, partially covered cubic shaped ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals on the surface of nanodisk were noticed, as shown in Fig. 3b-c. This observation was further confirmed with TEM analysis, as shown in Fig. 3d. The individual size of cubic shaped ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystal were noted between 300 nm and 500 nm, which are consistent with high magnified FESEM images. These results strongly authenticated the successful formation of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks with scattered cubic shaped nanocrystals due to the reprecipitation induced crystallization. In order to verify the elemental composition of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) techniques were performed, as shown in Fig. 4 . Two different spots were selected for analysis, an entire area of the image that covers both nanodisks and cubic shaped nanocrystals and another spot selected on the surface of nanodisks. Based on the SEM-EDS results, not much difference in the result was observed. Based on the EDS data, the ratio of Cs:Pb:Br in ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks was calculated to be 4:1:6, indicating the successful formation of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. In addition to the elemental ratio, the elemental mapping technique was performed, as shown Fig. S4 (supporting information). These results authenticated that the successful isolation of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 crystals via the reprecipitation induced crystallization method.

Fig. 5 illustrates an experimental setup to measure the UV shielding trend of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks dispersed polymer film. More specifically, a digital lux UV meter was used to measure the intensity of UV light passing through the polymer film. The polymer film was placed just above a digital UV meter ensuring the complete passage of light through the polymer film. A UV lamp or the daylight light (Sunlight) was used as a light source. A series of polymer film was prepared with increasing content from 7.5–25 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. Fig. 6 shows the UV shielding tendency of polymer films under the illumination of UV light source. The polymer film PP-1 itself shielded the UV light around 30%, compared to the pure polymer film (PP-0, 10%) without ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. It means 7.5 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks can capable to absorb ˜20% of UV light. Likewise, other polymer films, such as PP-2, PP-3, PP-4 and PP-5 absorbs the UV light intensity of ˜44, 61, 77 and 80%, as shown in Fig. 6a. A similar testing procedure was executed with different light source to correlate the result. Here, a daylight source was used to test the polymer films and results are in similar trend, as shown in Fig. 6b. The PP-5 polymer film containing 25 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks could shield the maximum of 83% of UV light. The presence of UV absorption peak (300–340 nm), which extends till 510 nm of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks can be explained as the reason for the higher UV-shielding tendency of the PP-5 polymer film, as shown in Fig. 2e [18]. Besides, the dispersion of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks was examined using an optical microscope. Fig. 7 (a–b) show the low and high magnified optical microscopic images of PP-5 containing 25 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks. The uniform dispersion of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks with scattered cubic shaped nanocrystals observed throughout the polymer film. This result confirmed that an excellent UV shielding tendency of polymer film was achieved due to a stronger UV absorption peak of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks with the dispersion capability in a polymer matrix.

In addition, the UV shielding performance of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks dispersed film was further evaluated by monitoring the UV light induced degradation of model dye, a RhB dye, as shown in Fig. 7c. The polymer film (PP-0 or PP-5) was placed on the top of the RhB dye solution containing photocatalyst and followed the standard procedure. After 3 h of UV light illumination, about 45% of RhB dye degradation was observed with PP-0 film covered dye solution, while UV-light induced dye degradation was largely suppressed by the PP-5 film, which allows to degrade only 15%, as shown in Fig. 7d. This observation authenticated that a strong UV shielding tendency of polymer film containing ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, which could protect the UV sensitive RhB dye. Moreover, these polymer film emit a strong green luminescent upon illumination of UV light during dye degradation testing, a photographic image was displayed as inset in Fig. 7d.

Fig. 8 a illustrates the schematic to specify the possible mode of down-converted green emission of the polymer film containing ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks by absorbing UV light. Based on the recent literature, one way of explaining the reason for the green emission of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks can certainly due to the inclusion of CsPbBr3 phase, as confirmed by XRD and XPS analysis [42]. In other words, since, the dispersed ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks has a strong capacity of absorbing UV light, it absorbs ˜ 80% of UV light from the source to down-convert into a strong green emission, as illustrated in Fig. 8a. The plausible mechanism for a strong green emission of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks via a down-conversion was shown in Fig. 8b. Here, the high energy UV light absorbed by the Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks were transferred to the conduction band of CsPbBr3 phase to emit lower emission of green light via down-conversion process. Thus, these kind of green emissive UV shielding material can be acknowledged as the emerging materials in field of fabricating protective emulsion, paints and coating.

A routine reprecipitation method was incorporated to isolate nanocrystalline ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks to disperse in a polymer matrix for fabricating a strong green emissive UV shielding polymer film. The series of polymer films were prepared by incorporating different weight fraction from 7.5 mg to 25 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, which proved an excellent UV shielding capability from 30% to 83% with increasing content of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, respectively. The UV-shielding capacity of polymer film was further verified by investigating the degradation trend of UV-light induced RhB dye. It was found that UV shielding polymer film was effectively control the dye solution by blocking UV light. A plausible mechanism was proposed to explain the strong green emission upon UV light illumination. It was confirmed that inclusion of CsPbBr3 phase responsible for the down-conversion of high energy UV light into low energy green emission. Therefore, a green emissive UV shielding polymer film can be recommended to use as UV light filter to protect UV light sensitive materials.

A routine reprecipitation method is introduced to isolate ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks for better dispersion in a polymer matrix. As-prepared polymer film shows an excellent UV shielding of 80% was witnessed in a polymer film containing 25 mg of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks under a UV lamp. The UV-shielding capability of polymer film was further verified by testing of UV light induced rhodamine B degradation and compared to pure polymer film. Furthermore, the polymer film emits a strong green due to a down-conversion property of ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanodisks, upon illumination of UV light. Thus, the development of green emissive UV shielding polymer film can be recommended to use as a packaging film, emulsion, paints and protecting coating etc.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was encouraged by the management of Sona College of Technology and financially supported by BK21 plus, National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (KRF-2017R1A2B3006141).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.porgcoat.2019.03.025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on UV-shielding mechanism of layered double hydroxide materials

The impact of aerosols on solar ultraviolet radiation and photochemical smog
hRAD30 mutations in the variant form of xeroderma pigmentosum
Comparison of the UV-degradation chemistry of polypropylene, polyethylene, polyamide 6 and polybutylene terephthalate

Evidence for large upward trends of ultraviolet-B radiation linked to ozone depletion

Cerium oxide nanoparticles, combining antioxidant and UV shielding properties, prevent UV-induced cell damage and mutagenesis
Carbon quantum dots decorated leaf-like CuO nanosheets and their improved dispersion for an excellent UV-shielding properties in polymer films
High-performance TiO2/polyacrylate nanocomposites with enhanced thermal and excellent UV-shielding properties
A convenient method for isolating carbon quantum dots in high yield as an alternative to the dialysis process and the fabrication of a full-band UV blocking polymer film
The shielding effect of nano TiO2 on collagen under UV radiation
Role of carbon quantum dots and film thickness on enhanced UV shielding capability of flexible polymer film containing carbon quantum dots/N-doped ZnO nanoparticles
Highly flexible and visibly transparent poly(vinyl alcohol)/calcium zincate nanocomposite films for UVA shielding applications as assessed by novel ultraviolet photon induced fluorescence quenching
Transparent nanocellulose hybrid films functionalized with ZnO nanostructures for UV-blocking
Cerium phosphate nanoparticles with low photocatalytic activity for UV light absorption application in photoprotection
Preparation and UV shielding property of Zr0.7Ce0.3O2 – kaolinite nanocomposites

Efficient monolithic perovskite/perovskite tandem solar cells

Ligand-mediated synthesis of shape-controlled cesium lead halide perovskite nanocrystals via reprecipitation process at room temperature
Colloidal synthesis of strongly fluorescent CsPbBr3 nanowires with width tunable down to the quantum confinement regime
High quality β-Ga2O3 film grown with N2O for high sensitivity solar-blind-ultraviolet photodetector with fast response speed
Precursor solution volume-dependent ligand-assisted synthesis of CH3NH3PbBr3 perovskite nanocrystals
Controllable and facile synthesis of CsPbBr3-Cs4PbBr6 perovskite composites in pure polar solvent

Aqueous synthesis of lead halide perovskite nanocrystals with high water stability and bright photoluminescence
Monodisperse and brightly luminescent CsPbBr3/Cs4PbBr6 perovskite composite nanocrystals
Ligand-free nanocrystals of highly emissive Cs4PbBr6 perovskite
Intrinsic lead ion emissions in zero-dimensional Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystals
One-step on-chip synthesis of highly-luminescent Cs4PbBr6 microcrystal
Synthesis of thermally stable and highly luminescent spherical shaped ligand-free Cs4PbBr6 nanospheres with a single polar solvent
Zero-dimensional cesium lead halides: history, properties, and challenges
